traveler_notes: Travelers from the US/Canada share Type A but should note Japan's lower 100 V. Visitors from the UK, EU and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: Japan runs at 100 V — the lowest mains voltage in the world. Most dual-voltage electronics cope fine, but check anything with a heating element.

traveler_notes: Travelers from the EU share Types C/F and usually need no adapter. Visitors from the US, UK and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: South Korea moved to the European-style 220 V round-pin standard; older buildings may still have Type A outlets.

traveler_notes: Type A suits US plugs, Type I suits Australian plugs, Type C suits much of Europe. UK travelers need an adapter.
quirk: Mainland China is unusually accommodating — it accepts three plug standards, but voltage is 220 V.

traveler_notes: US plugs (A/B) and European plugs (C) both fit many Thai sockets. UK and Australian travelers need a plug adapter.
quirk: Many Thai sockets are hybrid and accept both flat (A/B) and round (C) pins, but always at 220 V.

traveler_notes: US plugs (A) and European plugs (C/F) both fit common Vietnamese sockets. UK and Australian travelers need an adapter.
quirk: Vietnam's sockets often take both US-style flat pins and European round pins; voltage is 220 V.

traveler_notes: Travelers from the UK and Ireland share Type G and need no adapter. Visitors from the US, EU and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: Singapore uses the UK three-rectangular-pin Type G plug — handy if you're coming from Britain, an adapter for everyone else.

traveler_notes: European Type C plugs fit the smaller sockets. Travelers from the US, UK and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: India uses large round-pin Type D/M for higher-draw appliances; for phones and laptops a Type C adapter is usually enough.

traveler_notes: Travelers from Ireland and Singapore share Type G. Visitors from the US, EU and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: The UK's Type G plug is large and fused — distinctive, and not interchangeable with European round pins.

traveler_notes: Travelers from most of continental Europe need no adapter. Visitors from the US, UK and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: France uses Type E, which adds an earth pin to the standard European Type C outlet.

traveler_notes: Travelers from most of continental Europe need no adapter. Visitors from the US, UK and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: Germany's Type F 'Schuko' socket is the most common European standard and also accepts Type C plugs.

traveler_notes: Most European plugs fit, though Italy's Type L is distinctive. Visitors from the US, UK and Australia need an adapter.
quirk: Italy's Type L has three in-line round pins; many modern Italian sockets are hybrid and also take Type C/F.

traveler_notes: Travelers from most of continental Europe need no adapter. Visitors from the US, UK and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: Spain uses the standard European Type C/F round-pin sockets at 230 V.

traveler_notes: Type C plugs fit, but Switzerland's Type J is unique. Visitors from the US, UK, Australia and even some EU travelers need an adapter.
quirk: Switzerland's Type J socket is recessed and Swiss-specific — a standard Europlug (C) fits, but Type F 'Schuko' plugs often do not.

traveler_notes: Travelers from Canada, Japan and Mexico share Type A/B. Visitors from the UK, EU and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: The US runs at 120 V/60 Hz — visitors from 230 V countries should confirm their devices are dual-voltage (most phones and laptops are).

traveler_notes: Travelers from the US, Japan and Mexico share Type A/B. Visitors from the UK, EU and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: Canada matches the US standard: Type A/B at 120 V/60 Hz.

traveler_notes: Travelers from New Zealand and China share Type I. Visitors from the US, UK and EU need a plug adapter.
quirk: Australia's Type I plug with its angled flat pins is shared with New Zealand and parts of China and Argentina.

traveler_notes: Travelers from Australia and China share Type I. Visitors from the US, UK and EU need a plug adapter.
quirk: New Zealand uses the same angled-pin Type I plug as Australia, at 230 V.

traveler_notes: Travelers from the UK share the dominant Type G. Visitors from the US, EU and Australia need a plug adapter.
quirk: The UAE follows the British Type G standard in most hotels, a legacy of historic ties to Britain.

traveler_notes: European Type C plugs fit Brazil's Type N sockets. Travelers from the US, UK and Australia need an adapter — and should watch the voltage.
quirk: Brazil is the voltage trap: some regions run 127 V and others 220 V, sometimes within the same city. Always check the local label.

traveler_notes: South Africa's large Type M is distinctive; newer sockets add Type N. Travelers from everywhere typically need an adapter.
quirk: South Africa's Type M plug is unusually large; a dedicated South Africa adapter is worth buying before you go.
